title: The Macro Minute: Is US productivity growth still booming?

Happy Thursday out there, team 42, it's
your skipper here, Darius Dalebard, and
our macro minute for Thursday, May 7th,
2026. As always, we start with the
executive summary from today's lead-off
morning note, so let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, is US
productivity growth still booming?
Our answer is, no. The slowdown in
non-farm productivity growth did not
support our productivity boom theme. The
slowdown in unit labor costs gives us
confidence that Q1 non-farm productivity
growth will likely be revised higher,
and that the current strong negative
impulse condition in non-farm
productivity growth represents a
transitory slowdown.
As detailed in our May 2026 macro
scouting report, there are three key
reasons why we believe productivity
growth is in a structural uptrend,
cyclical fluctuations aside. Number one,
a recent historical president precedent,
rather, for a similar, yet less
productivity-enhancing technology as AI,
catalyzing a 150 basis point trend
acceleration in productivity growth.
Number two, leading indicators of
productivity growth accelerating to
historically high growth rates. And
number three, significant capital
deepening.
All told, we reiterate our productivity
boom theme, and would fade any calls for
a sustained downtrend in economic
growth, or a sustained uptrend in
inflation. In our view, the market has
this right.
Transition to our 42 macro dashboard. As
always, we wrap up with the question
from our community. This one's titled,
"Buffett's $400 billion in cash." It
says, "Darius, you mentioned yesterday's
morning note that you can't wait for our
yesterday's macro minute that you can't
wait for a pullback to just invest in
the trend." Uh sorry, let me read this
again. "Darius, you mentioned in
yesterday's macro minute that investors
shouldn't wait for pullbacks and to just
invest in the trend. What are your
thoughts with Warren Buffett selling
stocks and building cash for the past
four to five years? He has
He now has an estimated $400 billion in
three-month Treasuries. Is it wrong to
wait for a fat pitch opportunity?" So,
let me start by saying, you know, I'm
not going to say anything Warren Buffett
is doing is wrong. Uh that would be a
ridiculous a
coming from me. But the thing I will say
is that
you know, just from a mathematical
standpoint, uh waiting for fat pitch
opportunities is not the best solution.
And here's why.
Compounding works better with larger
sums of money.
What's a 10% return on a million
dollars?
What's a 10% return on 10 million
dollars? You do that math at home and
tell me which is a larger sum of P&L.
Bingo. So, the whole point of riding the
trend and using quantitative risk
management systems like our KISS or our
Dr. Mo's system to help you cut off the
left tail of the distribution so that
you don't have to participate in
downtrends allows you to compound
returns from higher net asset values.
When you compound returns from higher
net asset values, your portfolio goes up
faster because you are compounding with
a larger sum of money. And so, sitting
there waiting for a fat pitch
opportunity traps your portfolio at a
low level of money because you're not
participating in the uptrend uh in the
market. You want to participate in the
uptrend in the market so that when the
market goes up, let's say the market's
up 1% today, it's going up 1% on a
larger sum of money as opposed to
sitting there waiting for fat pitch
opportunities. You know, there's more
than one ways to skin a cat, but you
know, again,
the entire hedge fund industry that I'm
staring at here in New York and the
entire global hedge fund industry has
figured out uh that compounding works
better uh with larger sums of money and
avoiding volatility drag is the solution
to uh growing wealth uh fastest. So,
we're going to continue to do what we're
doing. Uh but you know, no no disrespect
to Uncle Warren. Obviously, he's one of
the best investors of all time, but uh
in our from our from our take from our
vantage point, sitting out entire market
cycles for 4 to 5 years at a time is is
no way to retire on top of comfortably.
Uh he has that luxury. Trust me, you
don't. So, we're wrapping up there.
Dairy still here presenting our macro
minute for uh Thursday, May 7th, 2026.
Uh best of luck out there today. Uh we
will be we'll catch back here on uh
Monday. Cheers.
